ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tawa-roasted spiced fish fillet; closest_parent 'fish food product' (FOODON_03301116) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP 'Fish, cooked' is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for tawa-roasted masala fish fillet in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226760005 'Fish dish (food)' is the closest available parent concept; no specific concept for tawa fish exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Tawa Fish consists of 3 pieces of masala-coated dori fish fillet, approximately 240 g total, as served at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Fish and Fish Products (Rohu, Catla, and similar lean white fish, cooked with spices)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Dori fish (Pangasius hypophthalmus) is a lean white fish widely used in Indian restaurant cooking in Singapore. ICMR-NIN values for cooked lean white fish (rohu/catla category) were used as the primary nutrient anchor and adapted to account for masala spice coating and tawa-roasting method. Spice contribution to macros is minor; sodium adjusted upward to reflect masala seasoning. USDA FoodData Central entry for Pangasius (catfish, cooked) was used as a cross-reference for protein and fat values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — protein-dominant fish and seafood dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tawa Fish. GI estimated at 30 based on analogous protein-dominant cooked fish dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Protein-dominant fish dishes with minimal starchy carbohydrate consistently show GI in the 25–35 range. Carbohydrate contribution is from masala spice coating only (~3.8 g per 100 g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 9.1) / 100 ≈ 2.7, where carbohydrate per 240 g serving = 9.1 g.

hypertension.guidance: Each 240 g serving provides approximately 1008 mg sodium, representing about 44% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tawa Fish has an estimated GI of 30 and a glycemic load of approximately 2.7 per serving, making it a suitable high-protein, low-glycaemic option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein with moderate unsaturated fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dori fish is a lean white fish with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio; regular consumption of lean fish is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk, though the sodium content from masala seasoning warrants attention in individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tawa Fish is an Indian spiced fish dish in which a lean white fish fillet — typically dori (Pangasius hypophthalmus, also known as basa) — is coated in a dry masala blend of chilli, turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger, and garlic, then roasted on a flat iron griddle called a tawa. The tawa imparts a characteristic slightly charred, aromatic crust while keeping the interior moist and flaky. The dish is a staple of North Indian and Mughlai restaurant menus and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, where it is commonly presented as a 3-piece serving. Nutritionally, Tawa Fish is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with a low glycemic index (estimated GI ~30), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The masala coating contributes modest carbohydrate (~3.8 g per 100 g) and dietary fibre (~0.8 g per 100 g). Sodium content is elevated due to seasoning (~420 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when prepared with permissible ingredients. It is a good source of vitamin B12, niacin, phosphorus, and selenium, consistent with the nutritional profile of cooked lean white fish.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tawa fish?
Is tawa fish good for diabetics?
What fish is used in tawa fish?

confidence_reason: Dori fish (Pangasius) is a well-characterised lean white fish with published nutrient data in ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central. Macronutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked lean white fish data cross-referenced with USDA Pangasius values, with sodium adjusted for masala seasoning. No exact ICMR-NIN entry for tawa-roasted masala dori fish exists, hence medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for protein-dominant fish dishes.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked lean white fish (rohu/catla category) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central Pangasius (basa) data. No exact published entry for tawa-roasted masala dori fish fillet was found in any approved primary source; values represent a defensible adaptation. 2. Sodium_mg (420 mg per 100 g) is elevated relative to plain cooked fish (~60–80 mg) to account for masala seasoning and salt used in restaurant preparation; this is an estimate. 3. Carbohydrate_g (3.8 g per 100 g) reflects the masala spice coating contribution; plain dori fish has near-zero carbohydrate. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero for lean fish cooked without hydrogenated fat. 5. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for protein-dominant cooked fish dishes; no direct GI measurement for tawa fish was found. 6. Serving size of 240 g (3 pieces) is estimated based on typical restaurant portion for a 3-piece fish fillet serving. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, and micronutrient values are sourced from ICMR-NIN and USDA cross-reference for cooked lean white fish. 8. Halal status is assumed based on the fish being a permissible species and typical Indian restaurant halal certification in Singapore; not independently verified for this specific restaurant. 9. The description references Zaffron Tandoori – Boat Quay as the source restaurant from field survey data data.
